ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for salmon donburi; mapped to the parent class 'rice bowl dish' (FOODON_00002809) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Rice dish) used as the closest defensible category; no specific donburi or salmon-rice-bowl code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for salmon donburi as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of salmon don is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 180 g of steamed white rice and 120 g of raw salmon slices arranged on top.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon Don (Raw) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for 'Salmon don (raw)' (record ID F05100119). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central salmon and white rice composite (see notes).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salmon donburi as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice (polished), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The salmon component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect the dish GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × available carbohydrate per serving (21.36 g/100g × 300 g serving = 64.1 g) / 100 = 46.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium at 15 mg/100g measured without soy sauce; potassium 88 mg/100g from salmon supports favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Salmon don as measured is very low in sodium (15 mg/100g) and is suitable for hypertensive patients provided soy sauce is used sparingly or replaced with a low-sodium alternative, as a single tablespoon of regular soy sauce can add approximately 900 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice dominates carbohydrate load (21.4 g/100g) with very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100g), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size, request brown or mixed-grain rice to lower the glycaemic index, and increase the salmon-to-rice ratio to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate balance and blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Raw salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids (estimated ~0.8–1.2 g/100g dish) with low saturated fat (~0.72 g/100g) and low cholesterol (13.3 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids from sashimi-grade raw salmon support cardioprotective lipid profiles by reducing triglycerides and platelet aggregation; patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid adding high-sodium soy sauce and consume this dish as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High EPA/DHA omega-3 content from raw salmon reduces joint inflammation; no cooking-induced AGEs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw sashimi-grade salmon is among the richest dietary sources of anti-inflammatory EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids; however, immunocompromised RA patients on biologics or methotrexate should consult their treating physician before consuming raw fish due to food safety risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon don (raw), also known as salmon donburi or raw salmon rice bowl, is a Japanese-origin dish consisting of sashimi-grade raw Atlantic or Pacific salmon slices arranged over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The dish is popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a casual Japanese restaurant and food-court staple. The rice base is warm, slightly sticky, and mildly seasoned, while the salmon is served cold and raw, offering a buttery, rich, and delicate flavour with a silky texture. Condiments such as soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger are typically served on the side. Per 100 g, the dish provides 149 kcal, 6.98 g protein, 3.46 g fat, and 21.36 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium (15 mg) as measured without soy sauce. The dominant macronutrient is carbohydrate from white rice, which has a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The raw salmon component contributes EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, which are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory, and provides high-quality complete protein. Dietary fibre is low at 0.3 g/100g, reflecting the white rice base. The dish is dairy-free and low in saturated fat. It is not suitable for individuals who must avoid raw fish, including pregnant women and immunocompromised patients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a salmon rice bowl?
Is raw salmon rice bowl good for diabetics?
Is salmon don high in sodium?
Can I eat salmon don if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of salmon donburi?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F05100119), which is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. The dish is an exact match. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite of salmon and white rice, documented in notes. GI assigned by analogy to Japanese white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.72 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.10 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.20 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.0 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw Atlantic salmon (NDB 15076) and cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (NDB 20444), weighted approximately 40% salmon and 60% rice by mass, as the HPB source does not report fatty acid breakdown. Trans fat is confirmed negligible for raw salmon and plain steamed rice per USDA. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this composite dish (F05100119) and detailed per-100g composite micronutrient data for this specific preparation are not available in the HPB database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008, Table 1). 4. Halal status is not confirmed; salmon is permissible but soy sauce may contain alcohol-based additives depending on brand. 5. The dish is described as 'raw' indicating no heat treatment of the salmon; food safety advisory applies for vulnerable populations.
